draft.introduction: Confessions Book 11 contains Augustine’s famous analysis of time: “What then is time? If no one asks me, I know; if I wish to explain it to one who asks, I do not know.” It remains one of the most penetrating philosophical examinations of temporality ever written.

translation.text: What then is time? If no one asks me, I know what it is. If I wish to explain it to one who asks, I do not know. Yet I say with confidence that I know this: if nothing passed away, there would be no past time; if nothing were coming, there would be no future time; if nothing were, there would be no present time.
translation.original_text: Quid est ergo tempus? Si nemo ex me quaerat, scio; si quaerenti explicare velim, nescio

translator_notes.note: Augustine’s resolution—that time exists as a “distension of the soul” (distentio animi), with past as memory, future as expectation, and present as attention—anticipates Husserl’s phenomenology of time-consciousness by fifteen centuries.
